coredata.dc:title: P-doped carbon quantum dot graft-functionalized amorphous WO3 for stable and flexible electrochromic energy-storage devices
coredata.dc:creator: Jo, Myeong-Hun
Kim, Kue-Ho
Ahn, Hyo-Jin
coredata.dc:description: The intrinsic structural instability of amorphous WO3 (a-WO3) following repeated Li ion insertion/extraction and bending hinders its application in stable and flexible electrochromic (EC) energy-storage devices. To resolve this problem, developing an elaborate heterostructure of a-WO3 is essential for high-performance flexible EC energy-storage devices. Herein, a novel structure is proposed to introduce P-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) into a-WO3 through a low-temperature process to induce robust chemical linkages with a-WO3. The functional groups of P-CQDs form strong hydrogen bonds with a-WO3 in the form of quantum dot grafts, which effectively tolerate structural deformation following long, repetitive cycling electrochemical reactions and bending. Moreover, the functional groups and graphitic carbon of P-CQDs improve the Li ion wettability and electrical conductivity, respectively, thereby enhancing the electrochemical activity and kinetics. As a result of the impacts, P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 (P-CQD/a-WO3) electrodes exhibit excellent EC energy-storage performances. Furthermore, a flexible EC energy-storage device fabricated with the P-CQD/a-WO3 electrode exhibits a remarkably enhanced long-term cycle stability (transmittance retention of 87.3% after 4,000 cycles) and flexibility (transmittance retention of 84.1% and specific capacitance retention of 85.3% after 500 bending cycles). Therefore, we believe that the proposed heterostructure-inducing quantum dot graft functionalization with a-WO3 is a breakthrough in the realization of high-performance flexible EC energy-storage devices.

Stable and flexible performance

(a) Fabrication process of P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 flexible electrode. Top-view SEM images (inset: high-resolution images) of (b) a-WO3, (c) CQD/a-WO3, and (d) P-CQD/a-WO3 films. (e) EDS images for the W, O, C, and P elements of the P-CQD/a-WO3 film.

(a-e) Chemical bonding state surveys of all films: (a) FTIR spectra in the range of 400–4,000 cm−1. XPS spectra of (b) W4f, (c) O1s, (d) C1s, and (e) P2p, and (f) resultant peak area ratios of –OH/W-O, C=O/C–C, C-OH/W-O, and C-OH/C–C obtained from the XPS.

(a) Photograph and schematic representation of the P-CQD graft-functionalization with a-WO3. (b-d) Demonstration of the QD graft-functionalization with Cs -TEM and EDS results of P-CQD/a-WO3 film: (b) EDS images for W, O, C, P, and N elements, (c) Cs -TEM image (inset: SAED patterns), and (d) high-resolution Cs -TEM image of selected area (black dotted square in (c)).

(a-e) EC performances of all flexible electrodes: (a) CV curves at a scan rate of 20 mV/s in a potential range of −0.7 to 0.3 V, (b) comparative bar graphs of DLi for anodic and cathodic diffusions, (c) in-situ transmittance curves at 633 nm under potentials of −0.7 V (coloration) and 0.3 V (bleaching) for 60 s, (d) comparative bar and line graphs of switching speeds (coloration and bleaching) and △T at 633 nm, and (e) optical density variations with applied charge density. (f) Schematic for the enhanced electrochemical behavior of P-CQD/a-WO3. (g and h) Energy-storage performances: (g) GCD curves at a current density of 2 A/g in a potential range of −0.7 to 0.3 V, and (h) specific capacitances at 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 A/g.

Long cycle stability and flexibility of flexible EC energy-storage devices made up of PEN//ITO//P-CQD/a-WO3//gel electrolyte//Pt//ITO//PEN: (a) Transmittance retention following consecutive 4,000 cycles under potentials of −2.5 V (coloration) and 2.0 V (bleaching), and (b) transmittance retention and capacitance retention following 500 bending cycles.

Summary of energy-storage performances of all flexible electrodes.

head.ce:title: P-doped carbon quantum dot graft-functionalized amorphous WO3 for stable and flexible electrochromic energy-storage devices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Seoul National University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The intrinsic structural instability of amorphous WO3 (a-WO3) following repeated Li ion insertion/extraction and bending hinders its application in stable and flexible electrochromic (EC) energy-storage devices. To resolve this problem, developing an elaborate heterostructure of a-WO3 is essential for high-performance flexible EC energy-storage devices. Herein, a novel structure is proposed to introduce P-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) into a-WO3 through a low-temperature process to induce robust chemical linkages with a-WO3. The functional groups of P-CQDs form strong hydrogen bonds with a-WO3 in the form of quantum dot grafts, which effectively tolerate structural deformation following long, repetitive cycling electrochemical reactions and bending. Moreover, the functional groups and graphitic carbon of P-CQDs improve the Li ion wettability and electrical conductivity, respectively, thereby enhancing the electrochemical activity and kinetics. As a result of the impacts, P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 (P-CQD/a-WO3) electrodes exhibit excellent EC energy-storage performances. Furthermore, a flexible EC energy-storage device fabricated with the P-CQD/a-WO3 electrode exhibits a remarkably enhanced long-term cycle stability (transmittance retention of 87.3% after 4,000 cycles) and flexibility (transmittance retention of 84.1% and specific capacitance retention of 85.3% after 500 bending cycles). Therefore, we believe that the proposed heterostructure-inducing quantum dot graft functionalization with a-WO3 is a breakthrough in the realization of high-performance flexible EC energy-storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Net-zero emissions of CO2 by humans are necessary to prevent a further increase in the global mean temperature. To realize this global objective, countries worldwide have accelerated the development of energy devices and technologies such as energy storage, conversion, and preservation technologies [1–3]. Smart windows, which are a type of electrochromic (EC) devices, have been extensively researched owing to their adjustable optical properties such as transmittance, reflectance, and absorbance. Smart windows can reduce the significant energy consumption for air conditioning and heating in buildings by modulating the solar heat flux [4]. In addition to the effective energy management properties, the applicability of recent EC devices has been expanded in the form of multi-functional energy-storage devices such as EC supercapacitors and EC batteries [5,6]. Typical EC devices have layered structures including a transparent conducting layer/cathodic layer/electrolyte/anodic layer/transparent conducting layer. Optical variations are generated from the redox reaction accompanying Li ions and electron transport at the active layers (cathodic and anodic layers) [4,7]. Owing to this characteristic, the EC device can simultaneously provide energy storage. Recently, a new generation of EC devices with enhanced flexibility has been studied to overcome the limitations of using conventional EC devices in portable and lightweight technologies [8–10]. However, there are still some challenges hindering the successful realization of flexible EC energy-storage devices that possess substantial flexibility, high cycle stability, and optimal EC indicators (transmittance modulation, switching speeds, and coloration efficiency).

Representative active materials for EC cathodic and anodic layers are classified as inorganic (transition metal oxide and Prussian blue-based materials) and organic (conjugated polymer and viologen) materials. The EC characteristics of inorganic materials are directly related to the physical mechanisms underlying the operation of EC materials. The color change of inorganic materials is generated by the double injection of ion species and electrons; by contrast, organic materials change color by an electroredox reaction [11]. Accordingly, inorganic materials possess significant advantages such as cyclic stability, a broad working temperature range, and chemical stability [12]. Despite these advantages, inorganic materials suffer from an inadequate cyclic stability, poor flexibility, and lower switching speeds compared to those of organic materials [13]. Thus, for the potential commercialization of EC devices, these drawbacks must be addressed while retaining the advantages of inorganic materials. Tungsten oxide (WO3), a transition metal oxide, has been widely applied as a cathodic material because of its favorable EC energy-storage properties such as optical modulation, specific capacitance, and coloration efficiency [4–6]. WO3 can be used in a crystalline or amorphous form, and the electrochemical behaviors of each form are significantly different. Crystalline WO3 has a relatively low ion capacity and weak kinetics, leading to critical flaws in EC energy-storage devices; this is related to its densely packed crystal framework. By contrast, amorphous WO3 (a-WO3) induces a low energy barrier for Li ion transport due to the loosely packed amorphous structure, leading to excellent electrochemical capacity and kinetics [14,15]. Nevertheless, a-WO3 entails intrinsic problems of structural stability pertaining to repetitive ion transport and bending flexibility because of the disordered and porous structure, which causes Li ion accumulation and atomic bond break off [16]. In this context, an effective approach to enhance the structural stability of a-WO3 should be developed to realize high-performance flexible EC energy-storage devices and their successful commercialization.

Various studies have been carried out to improve the structural stability of a-WO3. Zhao et al. prepared heterostructured WO3 films with controllable crystallinity by radio frequency (RF) magnetron sputtering [17]. The a-WO3 film with an optimized crystallinity exhibited remarkable transmittance modulation and cycle stability. However, to induce crystalline WO3 at the beginning of the RF sputtering, the substrate should be maintained at a high temperature (400 ℃), which is inappropriate for flexible substrates as the polymer is vulnerable at high temperatures (>250 ℃). In this regard, to ensure both cycle stability and flexibility without the performance degradation of EC energy-storage devices, designing a robust heterostructure of a-WO3 based on a low temperature process (<100 ℃) should be considered as a rational strategy for attaining high-performance flexible EC energy-storage devices. Carbon nanomaterials are gaining attention as an effective additive for various electrochemical electrodes due to their prominent physical and chemical properties such as high electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, and electrochemical stability [18]. Among them, graphene and carbon nanotubes have been used extensively because of their favorable electron conductance, dispersibility in active materials, and excellent physical stability against bending and volume expansion [19]. Recently, several studies highlighted the potential versatility of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the form of nanocomposites for various applications such as electrocatalytic water splitting, electrochemical biosensors, electrochemical supercapacitors and Li ion batteries [20–23]. However, abundant hydrophilic functional groups on CQDs mainly limit the dispersibility in organic solvents, leading to a heterogeneous distribution of CQDs with aggregates in the electrode matrix. Furthermore, no attempt has been made to induce an elaborate chemical linkage between the functional groups on CQDs and the matrix particles in the electrochemical electrodes. In this context, the introduction of robust chemical linkages between CQDs and a-WO3 could be a novel and effective method to improve the structural stability as well as charge transport of Li ions and electrons in flexible EC energy-storage devices.

In this paper, we first propose a P-doped carbon quantum dot (P-CQD) graft-functionalized a-WO3 electrode for flexible EC energy-storage devices. The entire spin-coating and annealing process for the fabrication of the flexible electrode is carried out at a low temperature (<100 ℃). Phosphoric acid, used as a P-doping source, induces an increased number of functional carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups, which are prone to form hydrogen bonds. The resultant functional groups of the P-CQDs form robust chemical linkages in the form of hydrogen bonds with a-WO3. Sturdy buffer quantum grafts can effectively alleviate structural deformation under both repetitive electrochemical reactions and bending conditions. Thus, the P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 electrode exhibits a remarkably high cycle stability and flexibility, which are suitable for flexible EC energy-storage devices. This novel heterostructure utilizing carbon quantum dots, which induces strong interactions with a-WO3, can provide a new perspective on the toleration of structural deformation, unlike existing conventional heterostructures.

CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid (C6H8O7, Aldrich) and urea (CH4N2O, Aldrich). The precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 2.673 g of citric acid and 0.9 g of urea in 30 ml of deionized water. In the case of P-CQDs, 0.36 ml of phosphoric acid solution (H3PO4, Samchun) was also added in the precursor solution as a P-doping source. After stirring for 1 h, the homogeneous solutions were transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the resulting CQD and P-CQD solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm; this was repeated twice to remove any impurities and excess reactants. The centrifuged solutions were dialyzed using a 6–8kD molecular weight cut-off membrane overnight to minimize the contents of impurities and obtain high-quality CQDs and P-CQDs. The dialyzed solutions were dried in an oven at 50 ℃ for 24 h, which resulted in CQD and P-CQD powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of flexible EC Energy-Storage electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: All the a-WO3 based flexible electrodes were fabricated by a simple process of spin-coating followed by low temperature annealing. The precursor solution for a-WO3 was prepared by dissolving 10 wt% of tungsten chloride (WCl6, Aldrich) in 2-propanol ((CH3)2CHOH, Aldrich) in an Argonne filled glove box. For the CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3, a specific filtration process was further applied by using membrane filtration. The 0.0066 g of synthesized CQD and P-CQD powders were dispersed in 2-propanol under 42 kHz ultrasonication for 6 h. Then, the solutions were filtrated using a syringe membrane filter (0.10 μm, JET BIOFIL), resulting in well-dispersed and transparent CQD and P-CQD solutions. Finally, CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 solutions were prepared by dissolving 10 wt% WCl6 in the CQD and P-CQD dispersions, respectively, in the glove box. The resultant precursor solutions were equally spin-coated two times on flexible ITO/PEN substrates at 2,000 rpm for 30 s in a humid-chamber, which was maintained at a relative humidity of 25%. Thereafter, all of the flexible electrodes were annealed at 80 ℃ for 1 h, resulting in a-WO3 films, as well as CQD and P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 films for EC energy-storage applications (named as a-WO3, CQD/a-WO3, and P-CQD/a-WO3).

The fluorescence properties of the synthesized CQDs and P-CQDs were investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Perkim-Elmer, Lambda-35), UV flashlight (UV-7 W, 395 nm), and confocal laser scanning microscopy (Carl Zeiss, 405 nm). The morphology and structure were observed via C s corrected scanning transmission electron microscope (C s -TEM, Jeol), high-resolution scanning electron microscope (HR-SEM, Hitachi), and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku). The optical properties were analyzed using UV–vis spectroscopy. The chemical bonding states were investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo fisher scientific) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab). The elemental composition and distribution were verified by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) mapping during SEM and TEM observations.

The EC performances were measured using UV–vis spectroscopy and a potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm autolab) simultaneously, based on a three-electrode system. To organize three-electrode system, the fabricated a-WO3 based flexible electrodes, a silver wire, a Pt wire, and 1 M of lithium perchlorate (LiClO4, Aldrich) in propylene carbonate (C4H6O3, Aldrich) were used as the working electrode, reference electrode, counter electrode, and electrolyte, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was investigated utilizing potentiostat/galvanostat with a sinusoidal signal of 10 mV amplitude under the frequency range of 0.1–100 kHz. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed at a scan rate of 20 mV/s in the range of −0.7 V to 0.3 V. The Li ion diffusion coefficients were calculated by surveying various scan rates of 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV/s. The chronoamperometry (CA) measurements were carried out by applying −0.7 V and 0.3 V for 60 s for each coloration and bleaching step. The energy-storage performances were evaluated by investigating galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves at different current densities of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 A/g. The long-term cycle stability test was performed based on a consecutive coloration and bleaching process (each step was measured for 30 s). The flexibility test of solid-state devices was conducted using an automatic-dynamic bending machine (ElectropulsTM, E3000). The flexible EC energy-storage devices were assembled with the a-WO3 based flexible electrodes as the working electrode, Pt flexible film as the counter electrode, and a LiClO4/PMMA/PC-based gel polymer electrolyte. The Pt flexible film was fabricated by spin-coating a precursor solution, which was composed of 4 M H2PtCl6·6H2O in terpineol, on flexible ITO/PEN films, followed by annealing at 100 ℃. The gel polymer electrolyte was prepared by dissolving 2.5 M of LiClO4 in the solvent, which comprised of propylene carbonate (PC, Aldrich) and acetonitrile (Aldrich). Then, 10 wt% of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Aldrich) was additionally added, and stirred in a bath at 50 ℃ for successful PMMA dissolution. Finally, the transparent gel polymer electrolyte was spread between the working and counter electrodes with a spacer, and the assembled electrodes were dried in a vacuum oven at 50 ℃ for 6 h, resulting in the flexible solid-state devices.

Inevitable issues such as structural instability following repetitive Li ion insertion/extraction and bending exist in a-WO3 based films employed in flexible EC energy-storage electrodes [11,12,14]. In this study, to counteract these limitations at once, a rational structure was introduced by inducing graft-functionalization of P-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) with a-WO3, which not only enhances the structural stability for long-term electrochemical reactions and bending, but also improves the electrochemical activity and kinetics of Li ions and electrons. Fig. 1 shows the optimal properties of the P-CQDs synthesized for graft-functionalization. The formation mechanism of the P-CQDs entails the thermal pyrolysis and condensation of citric acid, urea, and phosphoric acid under hydrothermal conditions. In the initial step, citric acid amide is formed via a thermal pyrolysis reaction between the citric acid and urea [24,25]. In addition, the phosphoric acid reacts with the epoxy group in citric acid, which forms a phosphodiester bond and hydroxyl groups via acid-catalyzed hydrolysis [26]. Afterward, the resultant phosphorus-doped citric acid amide self-assembles into a nanosheet structure and forms condensates following further heating. Consequently, P-CQDs are successfully formed via the growth of the condensates. C s -TEM surveys of the P-CQD powders (Fig. 1a and Figure S1) confirm the uniform distribution of quantum-scale carbon dots with a size range of ∼2–6 nm (average value of ∼4.29 nm) without aggregates. The lattice fringe spacing in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1b) reveals 0.205 nm, which corresponds to the (101) spacing of graphite carbon [27]. Furthermore, live fast Fourier transform (L-FFT) pattern and XRD pattern show the crystalline structure of P-CQDs with graphite (101) and (002) plane, respectively, which provides efficient electron conductance (Fig. 1c and Figure S2). As shown in the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of the P-CQD aqueous dispersion with a concentration of 25 mg/L (Fig. 1d), the P-CQD dispersion exhibits two specific absorption peaks at 202 and 333 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transition of graphitic sp 2 domains and n-π* transition of multi-conjugated C = O, respectively [28,29]. This graphitic and π-conjugated carbon structure of P-CQDs can provide efficient electron transfer channels, yielding a robust electrical conductivity. The fluorescence characteristics of the synthesized P-CQDs were directly investigated with a UV flashlight radiation and confocal laser scanning microscopy. As shown in the inset photograph in Fig. 1d, the P-CQD aqueous dispersion shows a reversible fluorescence switch between visible light and UV light. In addition, the confocal laser scanning microscope image of the P-CQDs exhibits a vivid blue-green fluorescence, which derives from the excitation of P-CQDs under the 405 nm laser radiation (Figure S3). Based on the above C s -TEM and fluorescence results, the synthesized P-CQDs, which exhibit a uniform distribution of quantum-scale carbon dots without aggregates, are likely to produce effective quantum scale grafting with a-WO3 particles for graft-functionalization.

Figure 2 a shows the straightforward fabrication process of P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 (P-CQD/a-WO3) flexible electrodes. Typically, abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups such as –COOH and –OH, which are located on the surface of CQDs, hinder their facile dispersion in organic-based solvents. The hydrocarbon parts of organic molecules provide lower polarity and Hansen solubility parameters than those of water, leading to CQD self-aggregation and low dispersibility (Figure S4a), which can result in inappropriate grafting with the a-WO3 host [30]. In this study, we solved this problem via a simple syringe-type membrane filtration of the P-CQD solution. We dissolved the P-CQD powders in 2-propanol using ultrasonication for 6 h, which yielded a green-black P-CQD suspension (the first photograph in Fig. 2a). The P-CQD suspension was filtrated using a syringe-type polyvinylidene fluoride membrane filter with a pore size of 0.10 μm (second photograph in Fig. 2a). Owing to the effective removal of possible self-aggregates of P-CQDs and residual organics in the P-CQD suspension, highly uniform quantum-sized P-CQDs are distributed in the P-CQD dispersion (Figure S4b); this enabled the robust quantum-grafting of the P-CQDs with a-WO3 particles during the spin-coating and low-temperature annealing process compared to that of P-CQD suspension. Accordingly, the resulting P-CQD dispersion exhibited a transparent pale-yellow color with excellent dispersibility (third photograph in Fig. 2a). As shown in Figure S5, the P-CQD dispersion exhibited fluorescence characteristics, which reveals that the QD particles in the 2-propanol solvent were homogeneously distributed, in contrast to case of the P-CQD suspension. WCl6 powder was then successfully dissolved in the P-CQD/2-propanol dispersion (fourth photograph in Fig. 2a). In the precursor solution, WOCl4 molecules are formed by spontaneous reaction between WCl6 and 2-propanol as followed Equation (1) and (2) [31,32]:

The resultant WOCl4 possibly serves as a trigger for the strong hydrolysis with ambient H2O molecules, resulting in amorphous WO3 with abundant hydroxyl groups during the spin-coating and low temperature annealing (80 ℃) process, as per the following reaction: WOCl4 + 2H2O → WO3 + 4HCl. Accordingly, the abundant functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl, on P-CQDs are prone to form hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups on a-WO3 particles, which induces strongly bounded chemical networks through the quantum dot grafts in the P-CQD/a-WO3 film structure (schemes in Fig. 2a). With the homogeneous distribution of chemically linked quantum dot grafts in the a-WO3 matrix, both CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 films display similar transmittance in the visible light region (average transmittance of 90.5% and 89.3%, respectively, in the range of 400–700 nm) when compared to that of the a-WO3 film (90.4%), as confirmed in Figure S7. Consequently, the P-CQD/a-WO3 flexible electrode displayed excellent EC coloration and flexibility, which will be discussed in detail in the following section (fifth photograph in Fig. 2a). Fig. 2b-d show top-view SEM images of a-WO3, CQD/a-WO3, and P-CQD/a-WO3 films, respectively. All a-WO3 based films display homogeneous and smooth surface morphologies, derived from the spontaneous hydrolysis reaction of WOCl4 with ambient water molecules during the spin-coating and annealing process [7,33]. In particular, as shown in the high-resolution SEM images (inset images with the orange dotted line), CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 exhibit densified morphologies compared to that of a-WO3; such densified morphologies were generated from the chemical linking between CQDs and a-WO3. The CQDs and P-CQDs contain abundant surface functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups (Figure S6); furthermore, a-WO3 can retain abundant –OH groups, which results from the animated hydrolysis reaction of WOCl4 following the low temperature annealing (80 ℃). The carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups of the CQDs and P-CQDs are prone to form strong hydrogen bonds with a-WO3 (–COOH∙∙∙OH–, –OH∙∙∙OH–, and C = O∙∙∙HO–), which leads to graft-functionalized linking in CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 [34–36]. This strong linking structure based on hydrogen bonds can grant flexibility and long-term cycle stability to flexible EC energy-storage electrodes. According to the investigation of EDS mapping images (Fig. 2e), the P-CQD/a-WO3 film shows a uniform distribution of W, O, C, and P, which demonstrates the elaborate P-CQD graft-functionalization with a-WO3. In addition, the elemental composition, obtained by the EDS images, confirms the relative percentages of the existing elements (Figure S8).

Figure 3 shows the chemical bonding states of a-WO3, CQD/a-WO3, and P-CQD/a-WO3. The infrared features were investigated using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 3a) and significant vibrational modes were detected. First, absorption peaks near 899.1 cm−1 and 609.2 cm−1 are commonly emitted by all samples, which are matched with the vibration modes of W-O-W and W-O, respectively [37]. The defects in WO3 usually activate the W-O vibrations in the range of 500–1000 cm−1, which confirms the amorphous structure [38]. Notably, the W-O peaks for CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 are shifted to a higher wavenumber (661.4 cm−1). This blue-shift of the W-O peak demonstrates strong chemical interactions, attributed to the hydrogen bonding between the CQDs and a-WO3 [39]. Additionally, the characteristic peaks at 1037.5, 1390.5, 1625.8, and 3347.1 cm−1, related to C-OH, COO–, C = C or C = O, and O–H, respectively, demonstrate the successful incorporation of CQDs and P-CQDs with a-WO3 [40–42]. The W4f XPS of all samples exhibit two main characteristic peaks at ∼35.97 eV for W4f 7/2 and ∼37.98 eV for W4f 5/2, which correspond to the binding energies of W6+ in the WO3 phase (Fig. 3b). The XRD patterns of both the a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 films exhibit a broad peak centered near ∼23.1°, which demonstrates the amorphous structure of the WO3, which can lead to favorable EC energy-storage performances due to the loosely bounded film structure that facilitates Li ion transport (Figure S9). The characteristic peaks at ∼34.97 and ∼37.28 eV correspond to the binding energy of W5+, which is related to the oxygen vacancies (VO) in the a-WO3 phase [4,7]. The presence of VO in a-WO3 improves the electrical conductivity of the film, which enhances the EC performance [43]. In this study, abundant VO were successfully retained in all samples because of the vigorous hydrolysis reaction of WOCl4 following the low temperature annealing process. However, the non-stoichiometric VO tend to induce Li+ accumulation and structural disorders after several hundreds of cycles, causing a significant reduction in transmittance modulation and capacity loss in EC energy-storage devices [4,15,44]. The robust hydrogen bonding achieved through the P-CQD graft-functionalization can efficiently offset this problem, while retaining the VO concentration, which can be estimated by calculating the relative peak area ratio of W5+/W6+ (0.147 for a-WO3, 0.146 for CQD/a-WO3, and 0.147 for P-CQD/a-WO3). The O1s and C1s XPS show particular chemical bondings as a result of the CQD and P-CQD graft-functionalization (Fig. 3c and d). According to the O1s XPS (Fig. 3c), all samples show peaks at ∼530.9 and ∼529.3 eV corresponding to W-O and VO bonding states, respectively. As expected, the peak area ratio for VO/W-O, which indicates the relative VO concentration in a-WO3, exhibits similar values (0.114) for all samples. Interestingly, in the cases of CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3, the –OH peak significantly shifts to a higher binding energy, even by 0.2 eV. This enhancement originates from the strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the CQDs and a-WO3 [45,46]. The hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups on the CQD and P-CQD surfaces form numerous hydrogen bonds with the –OH group of a-WO3, while a-WO3 exhibits few intermolecular –OH interactions. Moreover, the intermolecular –OH content, which can be estimated from the peak area ratio for –OH/W-O, of CQD/a-WO3 (0.51) is higher than that of P-CQD/a-WO3 (0.67). The enhancement in the intermolecular –OH content allows the introduction of more hydrogen bonds, which leads to the formation of a robust structure. During the synthesis of P-CQDs, phosphoric acid (used as a P-doping source) actively reacts with the epoxy group (C-O-C) of the carbon precursors, which results in the transformation of some epoxy groups to C-OH groups due to acid-catalyzed hydrolysis. Then, the reaction gradually deaccelerates as a phosphodiester bond (−P−O−C−) is formed between phosphoric acid and C−OH, thus preserving the C−OH group [26,47]. Consequently, the increased C−OH content of P-CQDs effectively triggers the formation of hydrogen bonds between the P-CQDs and a-WO3 molecules. In addition, the gradual increase in the C−OH content (C−OH/W−O) from CQD/a-WO3 to P-CQD/a-WO3 shows high coherency with the increased C−OH content of P-CQDs (0.19 for CQD/a-WO3 and 0.27 for P-CQD/a-WO3). As shown in the C1s XPS (Fig. 3d) of a-WO3, low-intensity peaks associated with C−C and C=O are observed at ∼284.5 and ∼287.2 eV, respectively, which attributed to the presence of organic compounds in the precursor. The C1s XPS of CQD/a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 exhibit four characteristic peaks related to C=C, C−C, C−OH, and C=O at ∼283.1, ∼284.5, ∼285.9, and ∼287.2 eV, respectively, which demonstrate CQD anchoring to a-WO3 [40,41,48]. The higher peak area ratio of C−OH/C−C in the spectra of P-CQD/a-WO3 than that of CQD/a-WO3 sugges-ts the enhancement in the hydroxyl group content of P-CQDs because of the phosphoric acid effect. Additionally, the carbonyl content (peak area ratio of C=O/C–C) of P-CQD/a-WO3 (0.32) is higher than that of CQD/a-WO3 (0.19). Phosphorus doping in CQDs can effectively stabilize carbonyl groups, which exist in a carboxylic acid state (C(=O)OH). The enhanced carbonyl content may induce electronic and structural reversibility during the repetitive Li ion insertion and extraction process, which attributes to the efficient electron transfer and high Li ion reversibility during Li ion insertion/extraction [49,50]. The characteristic peak in the P2p XPS (Fig. 3e) of P-CQD/WO3 indicates phosphorus incorporation, while a-WO3 and CQD/a-WO3 do not show the characteristic peak. To find out relative chemical bonding contents at a glance, the peak area ratios are summarized in the form of a bar graph (Fig. 3f). P-CQD/a-WO3 shows increased peak area ratios of –OH/W-O, C-OH/W-O, C=O/C–C, and C-OH/C–C, which indicates the occurrence of the elaborate graft-functionalization, owing to the effect of P-CQD anchoring. This can induce the long-term cycling stability and flexibility in flexible EC energy-storage devices owing to the structural stability and efficient charge transport of Li ions and electrons.

Figure 4 a depicts a schematic representation of the P-CQD graft-functionalization with a-WO3. Because of the P-CQD graft-functionalization following the low temperature process (80 ℃), the P-CQD/a-WO3 film fabricated on the ITO/PEN electrode exhibits superior flexibility and superior EC coloration (photograph in Fig. 4a). This is realized by the formation of a robust chemical linkages, which are achieved through the hydrogen bonds between P-CQDs and a-WO3. As shown in the molecular-level enlargement, P-CQD retains abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, particularly carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups, as a result of the phosphoric acid-catalyzed hydrolysis reaction. In addition, a-WO3 contains abundant hydroxyl groups owing to the spontaneous hydrolysis of WOCl4, which is formed by the reaction between WCl6 and 2-propanol in the precursor solution, with ambient water molecules during the spin-coating and annealing (80 ℃) process. On this basis, numerous functional groups spontaneously form strong hydrogen bonds between P-CQDs and a-WO3. The strong hydrogen bonds in the quantum-scale region may effectively tolerate structural deformation following bending and long-term Li ion insertion/extraction cycles. This novel approach can improve both flexibility and cycling performance of flexible EC energy-storage devices. The success of the quantum dot graft-functionalization was directly investigated by comparing C s -TEM images of the a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 films, as shown in Fig. 4b-d and Figure S10. For a precise and clear observation of the film structure, a-WO3 and P-CQD/a-WO3 films were each coated on a Si substrate, and all samples were observed after focused ion beam milling. As shown in Figure S10, the graphitic P-CQDs, uniformly distributed in the a-WO3 matrix, show a densified film structure owing to the formation of the chemical linkages in the form of hydrogen bonds with the a-WO3 particles compared to that of a-WO3 film. The uniform distributions of C, P, and O elements demonstrate uniform P-CQD distribution in the a-WO3 matrix as confirmed by the EDS surveys (Fig. 4b and Figure S11). Additionally, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of P-CQD/a-WO3 (inset image in Fig. 4c) reveals halo ring patterns that correspond to the amorphous structure of the a-WO3 matrix, while vague dot patterns are observed as a result of graphitic P-CQDs. In the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 4d) of the selected area in Fig. 4c, grafted P-CQDs (red dotted circle) are identified within the a-WO3 (blue dotted circle) in the film. Consequently, the use of the P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 electrode is likely to improve electrochemical performances of flexible EC energy-storage devices.

Figure 5 shows the multi-functional performances of flexible electrodes, fabricated with a-WO3, CQD/a-WO3, and P-CQD/a-WO3 films for EC and energy-storage applications. Fig. 5a shows CV curves from −0.7 to 0.3 V at a scan rate of 20 mV/s. All electrodes exhibit broad redox curves without specific peaks, which correspond to the conventional behavior of amorphous WO3. The redox reaction including the insertion/extraction of Li ions and electrons in the a-WO3 matrix generates a transmittance change between blue black and transparency (Equation (3)) [15]:
(3)

Notably, the CV curve area reveals an increasing tendency from a-WO3 to P-CQD/a-WO3, throughout the applied voltage range, which indicates the enhanced electrochemical activity of the electrode. The strong hydrophilic properties of the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups, which are derived from the anchoring of CQDs to a-WO3, improve Li ion wettability [51]. As a result, the enhancement in Li ion wettability near the negatively charged functional groups facilitates enhanced ionic accessibility, especially Li ion insertion/extraction, during the redox reaction, which leads to the enhanced electrochemical activity. Moreover, the intercalated CQDs in the a-WO3 matrix induce a larger interplanar spacing (d = ∼5.40 Å) between the amorphous WO3 lattices than a-WO3, which lowers activation energy for Li ion diffusion kinetics [16,52]. Therefore, P-CQD/a-WO3, wherein successful graft-functionalization of P-CQDs with a-WO3 particles occurs due to the increased content of functional groups, can effectively result in increased electrochemical activity and accelerated kinetics attributed to the enhanced ionic accessibility and reduced ionic diffusion barrier energy, respectively, during the electrochemical reactions. The accelerated ion and electron transport can be confirmed by the comparison of the EIS curves of the a-WO3, CQD/a-WO3, and P-CQD/a-WO3 electrodes (Figure S12). The Nyquist plots in the high-frequency region exhibit a semicircle, which is directly related to the charge transfer resistance for electronic conductance [53]. P-CQD/a-WO3 exhibits a smaller radius of the semicircle, which indicates a lower charge transfer resistance (4.83 Ω) than those of a-WO3 (12.40 Ω) and CQD/a-WO3 (6.00 Ω). The enhanced electrical conductivity of P-CQD/a-WO3 is attributed to the graphitic carbon structure that accelerates the electron transport along the sp2 domains with robust graft-functionalization of P-CQDs. In addition, a slope of the Nyquist plots in the low-frequency region indicates an ionic diffusion resistance called as Warburg impedance [54]. The steepest slope of P-CQD/a-WO3 demonstrates the accelerated Li ion transport kinetics, which is derived from the increased interplanar spacing to alleviate the activation energy of ionic diffusion. Accordingly, the P-CQD/a-WO3 electrode exhibits the highest Li ion diffusion coefficient (D Li), which is related to the accelerated electrochemical kinetics (Fig. 5b). The Randles-Sevcik equation is applied to calculate D Li (Equation (4)) [4,7]:

where Jp (mA/cm2) is the peak current density, D Li (cm2/s) is the Li-ion diffusion coefficient, C 0 (mol/cm3) is the active ion concentration in the electrolyte, and v (V/s) is the scan rate of CV curve. Figure S13(a-d) show the CV curves at stepwise scan rates and the resultant current densities for both anodic and cathodic reactions following v 1
/
2 for all samples. Based on the results of Figure S13, P-CQD/a-WO3 shows the highest Li ion diffusion coefficient (3.72 × 10-8 cm2/s for anodic diffusion and 2.37 × 10-8 cm2/s for cathodic diffusion) due to the increased interplanar spacing, which induces a lower activation energy for Li ion diffusion in the a-WO3 matrix. The EC performances, such as transmittance modulation, switching speeds, and coloration efficiency (CE), exhibit excellent consistency with the results of electrochemical activity and kinetics analyses through CV and DLi calculation, respectively, for all electrodes. The in-situ transmittance variations at 633 nm were measured through chronoamperometry (CA) under repeated potentials of −0.7 V (coloration) and 0.3 V (bleaching) for 60 s (Fig. 5c). Through the in-situ transmittance variation curves, transmittance modulation (△T) can be estimated by calculating the transmittance difference between the bleached and colored states at 633 nm. In addition, the switching speeds are defined by the coloration/bleaching times to reach 90% of △T. The results for all electrodes are summarized in Table 1 . CQD/a-WO3 exhibits an increased △T and high switching speeds as a result of CQD anchoring. The hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, which retain significant polarity owing to their negatively charged oxygen atoms, on the CQDs enhance the Li ion wettability, which promotes the ionic accessibility between the electrolyte and the CQD/a-WO3 film. In turn, the facilitated ionic accessibility encourages the increased quantity of Li ion insertion/extraction during the electrochemical reaction, which relates to the increased △T of CQD/a-WO3 electrode. The enhanced switching speeds of CQD/a-WO3 are attributed to the simultaneous effects induced by increased interplanar spacing and the presence of graphitic carbon structures, which are achieved through the chemically linkages between the CQD grafts and the a-WO3 particles. The increased interplanar spacing induces a lower activation energy for Li ion diffusion in the a-WO3 matrix, which facilitates accelerated Li ion insertion/extraction during the electrochemical reaction. The graphitic carbon structure of the CQDs allow an increase in the electrical conductivity along the sp2 domains, which accelerates the electrochemical kinetics. Thus, the simultaneous acceleration of Li ion and electron transport kinetics of CQD/a-WO3 enables higher switching speeds than those of a-WO3. Notably, for P-CQD/a-WO3, these effects are maximized owing to the higher content of functional groups on the P-CQDs, which provides robust chemical linkage networks for graft-functionalization, as confirmed by FTIR, XPS, and TEM results illustrated in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4. Thus, P-CQD/a-WO3 exhibits superior △T (62.5 %) and high switching speeds (4.4 s for coloration and 2.7 s for bleaching). These performances are higher than those of a-WO3 and CQD/a-WO3 (Fig. 5d). Another crucial factor that must be considered in the evaluation of the EC performance is the CE, which can be defined by the variation in the optical density (OD) with the applied charge density (Q/A) as given in Equations (5) and (6) [5]:
(5)

(6)

where T b (%) is the transmittance in the bleached state and T c (%) is the transmittance in the colored state. As shown in Fig. 5e and Table 1, P-CQD/a-WO3 exhibits the highest CE (76.1 cm2/C), which is attributed to the synergistic effects of the enhanced △T and switching speeds as a result of the improved electrochemical activity (CV curve area) and efficient charge transport (D Li and electrical conductivity), respectively. Fig. 5f depicts a schematic illustration of a feasible mechanism underlying the enhancement of the electrochemical behavior induced by P-CQD graft-functionalization with a-WO3. Based on the mechanism for the EC reaction accompanying Li ion and electron transport, all electrodes can simultaneously exhibit energy-storage properties. Fig. 5g shows galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves at a current density of 2 A/g in the potential range of −0.7 to 0.3 V, which demonstrate a pseudocapacitive ability of the electrodes. The charge/discharge time gradually increases from a-WO3 to P-CQD/a-WO3, which indicates an increased quantity of Li ion insertion/extraction; this result shows a great consistency with the △T values. To evaluate the energy-storage properties of all samples, we calculated specific capacitance (C sp) by Equation (7) [33]:
(7)
C
sp
= I /
mdV / d t
where, I (A) is the current, m (g) is the total mass of active film, dV (V) is the voltage window, and dt (s) is the discharge time. Accordingly, P-CQD/a-WO3 exhibits a higher specific capacitance (120.6F/g at 2 A/g) than a-WO3. Moreover, P-CQD/a-WO3 exhibits a remarkable high-rate energy-storage capability (specific capacitance retention at 10 A/g versus 2 A/g) than a-WO3 and CQD/a-WO3 (Fig. 5h and Table 2 ). P-CQD/a-WO3 displays high-rate capability of 73.8%, while a-WO3 and CQD/a-WO3 exhibit capabilities of 52.0% and 67.2%, respectively; the superior high-rate capability is attributed mainly to the enhanced electron mobility, which is derived from the robust functional groups that can form chemical linkages with the particles, thereby providing effective electron transfer channels. Considering that P-CQD graft-functionalization induces strong hydrogen bonding with the a-WO3 matrix, the P-CQD/a-WO3 electrode is likely to retain the high long-term cycling stability and flexibility and can be used in high-performance EC energy-storage devices.

Figure 6 shows the long-term cycling stability and flexibility of the flexible EC energy-storage devices. To fabricate the flexible EC energy-storage devices, each component is strongly assembled, including the P-CQD/a-WO3 film on ITO/PEN as the working electrode, the Pt film on ITO/PEN as the counter electrode, and the LiClO4/PMMA/PC polymer gel as the electrolyte. The detailed fabrication process is presented in Methods. Fig. 6a shows the transmittance retention of the devices, following a consecutive coloration/bleaching process for 4,000 cycles. The coloration and bleaching processes are carried out at applied voltages of −2.5 and 2.0 V, respectively. As shown by the transmittance variation at 633 nm following 4,000 cycles (inset graph in Fig. 6a), the flexible EC energy-storage devices exhibit a △T of 59.3% at the first cycle, which almost reflects the EC modulation of the P-CQD/a-WO3 electrode. Upon cycling, the device exhibits a slight modulation degradation. Nevertheless, the device maintains a remarkable transmittance retention (87.3%) after 4,000 consecutive cycles. The actual coloration/bleaching states of the devices after the 4,000th cycle (inset photographs in Fig. 6a) demonstrates a high EC capability (△T of 51.7 %). The outstanding long-term cycling stability of the EC device is attributed to the robust structure of P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3, which possesses a chemically linked network of strong hydrogen bonds. The sturdy buffer quantum grafts of P-CQDs alleviate the structural deformation such as volume change and lattice distortion during repetitive Li ion insertion/extraction of the electrode. Moreover, the chemical linkage between a-WO3 with the help of P-CQDs can serve as the electron transfer pathway for the redox reaction following long-term cycling. In contrast, the flexible device made from the a-WO3 electrode exhibits a definite difference in the transmittance retention following consecutive EC cycling (Figure S14a and b). The flexible EC device fabricated with the a-WO3 exhibits a rapid decrease in △T as the cycling progresses, showing a transmittance retention of 62.37%, even after 2,000 consecutive cycles. This result is attributed to the structural deformation and irreversible Li ion accumulation in the a-WO3 film during the repetitive Li ion insertion/extraction. In this regard, the chemically linked buffer quantum grafts of the functionalized P-CQDs are expected to improve the flexibility of the EC energy-storage devices. Fig. 6b shows transmittance retention and specific capacitance as a function of the number of bending cycles of the devices. As shown in the inset photographs in Fig. 6b, one bending cycle is carried out followed by a sequence of bending (radius of curvature: 1.3 cm) and flattening. The flexible device exhibits remarkable transmittance retention (84.1 % at 633 nm) and specific capacitance retention (85.3 % at 2 A/g) after 500 bending cycles. Figure S15 shows the ex-situ EC and energy-storage results with transmittance variation in the range of 350–900 nm and the GCD curves after the 1st and 500th bending cycles. The dense and continuous morphology of the P-CQD/a-WO3 electrode can effectively strengthen the mechanical tolerance of micro-sized crack formation [55]. More importantly, chemically bounded networks in the a-WO3, which are achieved through the strong hydrogen bonds of quantum dot grafts, have a considerable impact on the repetitive bending deformation. To demonstrate the effects of P-CQD graft-functionalization on the properties of P-CQD/a-WO3, the flexibility of the devices made from the a-WO3 electrode is also investigated; the results are shown in Figure S14c and d. The flexible device fabricated with the a-WO3 exhibits a drastic reduction in specific capacitance, showing a capacitance retention of 63.7% even after 300 bending cycles. The inferior flexibility of the a-WO3-based device is attributed to the loosely bounded amorphous structure, which can be easily collapsed under a given bending condition. Furthermore, the flexible EC energy-storage device fabricated with the P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 electrode shows remarkable performances in both long-term cycling stability and flexibility in relation to previously reported devices fabricated with WO3-based electrodes (Figure S16 and Table S1) [56–63]. Our study is the first to suggest the formation of a strong chemical linkage among a-WO3 through anchoring of graft-functionalized carbon quantum dots following the low temperature process. Thus, this study will contribute to the development of novel EC energy-storage devices with simultaneously improved the long-term cycling stability and flexibility.

We developed a P-CQD graft-functionalized a-WO3 film that simultaneously allows robust charge transport and structural stability via a low temperature process. The hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups of P-CQDs effectively form strong hydrogen bonds with a-WO3, which induce the formation of a robust chemical linkages. The resultant chemically linked networks by quantum dot grafts not only facilitate Li ion and electron transport, but also tolerate structural deformation upon repetitive electrochemical reaction and bending. Therefore, the P-CQD/a-WO3 flexible electrode exhibits enhanced EC performances, including transmittance modulation (62.5% at 633 nm), switching speeds (4.4 s for coloration and 2.7 s for bleaching), and high CE (76.1 cm2/C at 633 nm), owing to the anchoring of CQDs, which increase the Li ion wettability, diffusivity, and electrical conductivity. The Li ion wettability was confirmed to originate from the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl functional groups; the Li ion diffusivity is influenced by increased interplanar spacing; and the electrical conductivity is mainly affected by conductive graphitic carbon. Notably, the flexible EC energy-storage device fabricated with the P-CQD/a-WO3 electrode exhibits outstanding long-term cycling stability (transmittance retention of 87.3% after 4,000 cycles) and flexibility (transmittance retention of 84.1% and specific capacitance retention of 85.3% after 500 bending cycles). Our study offers new insights into the design a robust structure that can tolerate structural deformation owing to carbon quantum dot graft-functionalization effects. Unlike the conventional heterostructures with structural stability, novel electrodes based on chemically linked structures derived from quantum dots through the proposed innovative structure design that can tolerate deformations such as lattice distortion, volume change, and particle fracture can be applied in the fabrication of long-life devices.
